what's the logic behind marking something as ratified or not?
Searched for "ratified" in the codebase and found nothing; asked for more context about the term.
Reiterated that "ratified" doesn't appear anywhere in the codebase and asked what it refers to.

], "ratified": false }, { "assertion": "A chain REPL client should demonstrate chain functionality by connecting to multiple relays under the same chain, identifying divergences between them, and providing reconciliation capabilities", "evidence": [ { "end": 843, "start": 843 } ], "ratified": false

Explained that `ratified: true` requires an assistant proposal later endorsed by the user, while `ratified: false` applies to user-stated claims or unendorsed assistant proposals; noted the field is metadata for review filtering since unratified claims are no longer dropped.

yes when processing /Users/pablofernandez/.claude/projects/-Users-pablofernandez-Work-serial-killer/572ae903-53ae-4432-9351-b771c4e244f7.jsonl it decided [...] some of those are false positives, don't you think? what can we do to improve it?

Identified two false-positive types: same-evidence duplicates and aspirational single-line "should" claims. Suggested a prompt fix to EXTRACT_PREAMBLE, post-process dedup, or a confidence flag for review.
as you can see, many of those are false positives, like "The relay implements NIP: Relay-enforced event chains"," -- I clearly stated that one myself

Clarified that all 23 claims correctly show `ratified: false` because the transcript has no assistant turn (only an API error), so ratification can never fire; the real issue is claim quality, not ratification.
what diference does that make? I said that, so it is ratified by me, even if the thing didn't run; who cares, the extract llm that is processing MY message it should mark that as ratified, bro
Acknowledged the valid point that user-stated claims should be `ratified: true` since the user is the authority; the current design conflates user-stated and unendorsed agent claims under `false`.
Agreed that `ratified` is the right word and the bug is in the implementation — the field should be `true` whenever the user is the authority behind a claim.
yes, fix it, commit and push
Pushed the fix; noted pre-existing untracked wiki files in the stash were unrelated.
add a pc debug transcript --all that will use the current claude code's PWD path to get all files and generate transcript for all conversation files for the current PWD
Pushed `pc debug transcript --all`, which resolves the project root and scans ~/.claude/projects/ for all matching sessions, printing each numbered transcript in mtime order.
Done. `pc debug extract --all` scans all sessions matching the current CWD and runs the full EXTRACT pipeline on each in mtime order.
